metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ACs (or extend AC3) requiring: (a) a test that simulates job-timeout (`REBAR_OPCERT_JOB_TIMEOUT_SECONDS=1` + a worker that sleeps) and asserts `status="error", class="timeout", verdict=null, envelope=null`; and (b) a test that raises an unexpected exception in the worker and asserts `status="error", class="internal"`; covering all four closed `error.class` values.
findings.finding: The closed `error.class` enum includes `"timeout"` (job exceeds `REBAR_OPCERT_JOB_TIMEOUT_SECONDS`) and `"internal"` (unexpected exception), but no AC requires a test asserting either mapping. AC3 covers `LLMError`→`"llm_error"` and `LLMUnavailableError`→`"llm_unavailable"` but is silent on the remaining two classes. A job-timeout or unexpected-exception in production would surface an unmapped/missing error class to clients, silently breaking the contract.
findings.location: AC3 (per-kind verdict mapping) and spec body: `error.class` closed enum (`"timeout"`, `"internal"`)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3: 'additionally, a job whose worker exceeds REBAR_OPCERT_JOB_TIMEOUT_SECONDS yields status="error", class="timeout", verdict=null, envelope=null (test with a 1-second timeout and a sleeping stub); an unexpected exception in the worker yields status="error", class="internal".'

findings.finding: AC7 states 'a test asserts the Dockerfile declares both' (OpenSSH ≥ 8.9 installation and service startup), but asserting a declaration in Dockerfile text does not verify the actual built container runs OpenSSH ≥ 8.9 — a parse of `RUN apt-get install openssh-client` with any version string would satisfy the test even if the pinned version is < 8.9. This is a fixture-validity gap (verify-command defect type 4).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert installs OpenSSH ≥ 8.9 and starts the service; a test asserts the Dockerfile declares both.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Dockerfile: `RUN ssh-keygen -V 2>&1 | grep -E 'OpenSSH_([89]\.[9-9]|[9-9]|[1-9][0-9])' || (echo 'OpenSSH >= 8.9 required' && exit 1)` — this makes the Docker build itself the falsifiable version check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move the `sign_completion_verdict` extraction from `transition_close.py` out of this story's scope — either assign it explicitly to the sibling producer-seam story (8d8e), which the parent already owns `transition_close.py` for, or update the parent epic's decomposition to re-assign this boundary before proceeding with this leaf.
findings.finding: The leaf modifies `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` (extracting `sign_completion_verdict`) as part of ee0b's scope, but `transition_close.py` is explicitly assigned to sibling story 8d8e (the producer signing seam) by the parent's decomposition — the parent says 8d8e handles 'the close-gate producer: the completion-verifier sign call in transition_close.py' and lists `transition_close.py` in 8d8e's `file_impact`. The parent's decomposition for ee0b is 'FastAPI async job handler, authoritative-state fetch, per-kind dispatch + verdict mapping, Secrets-Manager key materialization, container image, and the client command' — no modification of close-gate library code.
findings.location: What section — 'Signing = the producer seam, once; the service only PROVISIONS the key' sub-bullet; leaf Scope 'In' list
findings.suggested_fix: Revise the leaf's What section to remove the `sign_completion_verdict` extraction. Instead, the worker should invoke the same seam (`signing.sign_opcert_manifest` / `sign_manifest`) directly — as 8d8e already wires it — rather than calling a new function extracted from `transition_close.py`. The completion-verifier producer path in the service can call the signing seam directly without needing a shared wrapper that lives in transition_close.py's module. If the shared function is genuinely necessary, add it to 8d8e's scope (which already owns `transition_close.py`) and have ee0b depend on it as a contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that exercises `transition_close.py`'s completion-signing path end-to-end after the `sign_completion_verdict` extraction, asserting the signed cert produced by the close-gate is identical in structure to the pre-refactor output (e.g. a regression test that calls the close-gate path and validates the SIGNATURE event it appends).
findings.finding: The plan extracts `sign_completion_verdict` from `transition_close.py` into a shared function — a live code-path refactor — but no AC verifies that the existing close-gate completion flow (`transition_close.py` → `sign_completion_verdict`) still works correctly after the extraction.
findings.location: What section: 'this story EXTRACTS that pair into a reusable sign_completion_verdict(result, ticket_id, repo_root) used by BOTH transition_close.py (behavior unchanged) and this worker'; Acceptance Criteria: no corresponding AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'Regression: the existing `transition_close.py` completion-signing path is covered by a test that invokes the close-gate flow after the `sign_completion_verdict` extraction and asserts the appended SIGNATURE event is valid and structurally identical to pre-refactor output; `make test` must include this test.'

findings.finding: `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID` is used by the worker as the principal injected into the gate-run subprocess environment, but the plan never lists it as a service-level environment variable that must be present in the service's own runtime environment. An executor implementing the service will not know where this value comes from (SSM? hardcoded? deploy-time env var?).
findings.location: What > Signing section: 'sets REBAR_OPCERT_KEY_PATH to that path and REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID as the principal for the gate run'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the env-var list: '`REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID`: the logical environment id used as the DSSE keyid / SSHSIG principal; a deploy-time env var set by the IaC story (e.g. `nava-opcert-prod-1`); the worker passes it to the gate-run subprocess as-is.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the access class of `GET /opcert/jobs/{job_id}` explicitly (guarded or public-with-rationale) and extend AC 8 to assert the X-Opcert-Guard check applies to ALL /opcert/* endpoints, not only to the POST enqueue path.
findings.finding: The GET /opcert/jobs/{job_id} endpoint has an undeclared access posture. AC 8 (origin guard) scopes its enforcement to 'before any work is enqueued', which covers POST but leaves GET's auth requirement ambiguous. If GET is unauthenticated, anyone who can reach the service and guess or enumerate a job_id can read job results (verdict, material_fingerprint, merged_log_commit, error details).
findings.location: What section — async job API description + AC 8 (origin guard test)
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC 8 description change 'before any work is enqueued' to 'on all /opcert/* endpoints' and add a test asserting GET /opcert/jobs/{id} returns 403 for a missing/invalid guard header. Also specify that job IDs are cryptographically random UUIDs (UUID4) so enumeration is infeasible even if GET were accidentally exposed.

findings.finding: The access posture of GET /opcert/jobs/{job_id} is undeclared: the plan says 'the app additionally requires a shared secret' (implying app-wide middleware) but the guard AC is scoped to 'before any work is enqueued' (POST path only). It is ambiguous whether the GET status endpoint enforces X-Opcert-Guard. Job status responses include DSSE envelopes, verdicts, and material fingerprints — information that should not be accessible without authentication. If the guard is middleware it covers both; if it is a POST-only decorator it does not.
findings.location: AC: 'Origin guard' bullet; What section: 'GET /opcert/jobs/{job_id} returns the job record'; 'the app additionally requires a shared secret'
findings.suggested_fix: Apply the X-Opcert-Guard check as FastAPI middleware (not a per-route dependency) so it fires for every request regardless of method, and add a single AC assertion: 'GET /opcert/jobs/{id} without X-Opcert-Guard returns 403'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the module that will own the extracted `sign_completion_verdict` function (e.g. `rebar.llm.completion` or a new `rebar.opcert_service.producers`) so that both `transition_close.py` and the service worker can import it without ambiguity.
findings.finding: `sign_completion_verdict(result, ticket_id, repo_root)` is described as a new function this story extracts from `transition_close.py`, but the plan never specifies which module it will live in. Both `transition_close.py` and the service worker need to import it, but an executor must invent its home module.
findings.location: What section — 'Signing = the producer seam, once' paragraph; 'this story EXTRACTS that pair into a reusable sign_completion_verdict'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence under the 'Signing = the producer seam' paragraph: '`sign_completion_verdict` is extracted into `rebar.llm.completion` (next to `verify_completion`), imported by both `transition_close.py` and the service worker.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare a new `opcert` (or `opcert_service`) extra in `pyproject.toml` listing `fastapi`, `uvicorn[standard]`, and `boto3` as its deps, and name this extra in the Dockerfile.opcert `pip install` command in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan never declares a `pyproject.toml` extra for the opcert service, yet the service needs `fastapi`, `uvicorn[standard]`, and `boto3` (for SSM key fetching) at a minimum. The Dockerfile.opcert will need `pip install '.[<extra>]'` analogous to the reviewbot Dockerfile's `pip install '.[agents,reviewbot]'`. Without a named extra, the Dockerfile install command is underspecified and will either install wrong deps or silently fall back to base deps that omit FastAPI.
findings.location: Service section — 'Container image: infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert'; Scope/In list
findings.suggested_fix: Add `opcert = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "boto3"]` to `[project.optional-dependencies]` in `pyproject.toml`, and specify `pip install '.[agents,opcert]'` in the Dockerfile section of the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the required isinstance check order for the `error.class` dispatch: `LLMUnavailableError` (including its subclasses) must be tested BEFORE the broader `LLMError` catch, to prevent LLMConfigError from being misclassified.
findings.finding: The `error.class` exception-to-enum mapping specifies which exception types map to which enum values but does not specify the isinstance check order. `LLMConfigError` is a subclass of `LLMUnavailableError` which is a subclass of `LLMError`; without an ordered dispatch ladder, an implementer checking `LLMError` first will incorrectly classify `LLMConfigError` (and any other `LLMUnavailableError`) as `"llm_error"` instead of `"llm_unavailable"`.
findings.location: What section — 'Per-kind dispatch + native verdicts' paragraph: 'error.class is a CLOSED enum mapped from the raised exception type'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the error-class paragraph: 'Check order: (1) `isinstance(exc, LLMUnavailableError)` → `"llm_unavailable"`; (2) `isinstance(exc, LLMError)` → `"llm_error"`; (3) timeout branch; (4) else `"internal"` — LLMUnavailableError must precede LLMError because LLMConfigError is a subclass of LLMUnavailableError.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific SigV4 signing mechanism (e.g., `botocore.auth.SigV4Auth` + `botocore.awsrequest.AWSRequest`, or a named third-party package) and add it to the appropriate `pyproject.toml` optional-dependency group so the dependency is declared and the drift gate catches any omission.
findings.finding: The client `rebar remote-cert` is specified to 'SigV4-sign the request with ambient AWS credentials', but no SigV4 signing library is named, and a codebase search finds zero existing SigV4 HTTP-signing code or a matching dependency (`requests-aws4auth`, `aws-requests-auth`, botocore's `AWSRequestsAuth`). This creates risk that the implementor writes a bespoke SigV4 signer — a textbook NIH anti-pattern — rather than reusing an available library.
findings.location: Client section: 'SigV4-signs the request with ambient AWS credentials, submits the job, polls to a terminal status'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `botocore` (already required by the SSM SecureString fetch) — `botocore.auth.SigV4Auth` + `botocore.awsrequest.AWSRequest` + `botocore.credentials` provide a complete in-stdlib-of-boto SigV4 signer with no additional package needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific SigV4 HTTP-signing approach and add any required dep (e.g. `botocore.auth.SigV4Auth` + `botocore.awsrequest.AWSRequest` from the already-declared `boto3`, or `requests-aws4auth` declared in a new `opcert` extra) so the implementer doesn't have to discover it independently.
findings.finding: The client command `rebar remote-cert` is specified to 'SigV4-sign the request with ambient AWS credentials' but no library, API, or approach for SigV4 HTTP-request signing is named anywhere in the plan or codebase. The codebase has zero SigV4 HTTP-signing code today; `boto3` is present only for CloudWatch metrics publishing. The established Python approaches — `botocore.auth.SigV4Auth` (internal, undocumented boto3 API) or `requests-aws4auth` (separate third-party dep, not declared in `pyproject.toml`) — are both real, but neither is identified. An implementer must independently discover the approach, risking either an undeclared new dep or reliance on an internal API that has no stability guarantees.
findings.location: Client section: 'SigV4-signs the request with ambient AWS credentials'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `botocore.auth.SigV4Auth` + `botocore.awsrequest.AWSRequest` from the `botocore` package that ships with `boto3` (already in the `reviewbot` extra). Name this in the Client section and confirm the `opcert` extra will declare `boto3`. This avoids a new dep while staying on a public-enough internal API. A one-liner example: `auth = botocore.auth.SigV4Auth(credentials, 'execute-api', region); req = botocore.awsrequest.AWSRequest(method='POST', url=url, data=body); auth.add_auth(req)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name and declare the SigV4 HTTP-request signing mechanism for `rebar remote-cert` (e.g. `botocore.auth.SigV4Auth` + `botocore.awsrequest.AWSRequest` from an explicit `botocore` dep, or `requests-aws4auth`, or hand-rolled) and add it to the appropriate `pyproject.toml` extra.
findings.finding: The client `rebar remote-cert` command must SigV4-sign HTTP requests, but no SigV4 HTTP signing library is declared in `pyproject.toml` and no existing SigV4 signing code is present anywhere in `src/`. The only SigV4-capable dep in the repo is `boto3` (under the `reviewbot` extra), and `botocore.auth.SigV4Auth` can sign requests, but this is not named. `httpx` is installed in the env. Neither `requests-aws4auth` nor `botocore`-based signing nor any other approach is specified. This is a first-use integration with no implementation path named.
findings.location: Client section — 'SigV4-signs the request with ambient AWS credentials'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `botocore.auth.SigV4Auth` + `botocore.awsrequest.AWSRequest` (botocore ships with boto3, already in the `reviewbot` extra). Declare a new `opcert` extra in pyproject.toml that includes `boto3` (which pulls botocore), and document the signing pattern in the plan's Client section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default client poll interval and client-side give-up timeout (e.g. 'polls every 3 s, gives up after `REBAR_OPCERT_JOB_TIMEOUT_SECONDS + 60` s') so the executor has no free variable to invent.
findings.finding: The client `rebar remote-cert` command 'polls to a terminal status' but no poll interval or client-side max-wait timeout is specified, leaving the executor to invent a value.
findings.location: Client section: 'submits the job, polls to a terminal status'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Client paragraph: 'polls every 3 s; client-side give-up = `REBAR_OPCERT_JOB_TIMEOUT_SECONDS` + 60 s (env-configurable via `REBAR_OPCERT_POLL_TIMEOUT_SECONDS`); exits non-zero with class `timeout` if the ceiling is reached before a terminal status.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID` ↔ `trusted_environments.yaml` `env_id` matching invariant in a durable artifact (ADR entry, CLAUDE.md ops-note, or deployment runbook section) so it survives beyond this ticket and is discoverable by future operators.
findings.finding: The `trusted_environments.yaml` `env_id` ↔ DSSE keyid principal-matching constraint is a critical consumer-facing correctness contract ('MUST exactly equal the environment's env_id entry… it becomes the DSSE keyid the merge gate pins on'), but no operator runbook, ADR, design-doc section, or CLAUDE.md entry is planned to capture it. It exists only as inline prose in this ticket.
findings.location: What / Service section — 'REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID as the principal… MUST exactly equal the environment's env_id entry in the consuming project's .rebar/trusted_environments.yaml'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short ADR or CLAUDE.md section titled 'opcert service: env_id / principal contract' that states: the value of REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID must be an exact string-match for the env_id field in every consuming project's .rebar/trusted_environments.yaml; mismatches cause silent merge-gate failures.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a maximum queue depth (e.g. `asyncio.Queue(maxsize=N)`) and define the 429/503 behavior when the queue is full, so memory growth is bounded.
findings.finding: The in-process asyncio.Queue has no stated depth bound. A burst of POST /opcert/jobs requests can grow the queue without limit, consuming unbounded memory until the single serial worker drains it.
findings.location: What — Async job API section: 'POST /opcert/jobs validates, enqueues on an in-process asyncio.Queue'
findings.suggested_fix: Set `asyncio.Queue(maxsize=50)` (or a configurable `REBAR_OPCERT_MAX_QUEUE_DEPTH`); return HTTP 503 with `Retry-After` when the queue is full.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a retirement/eviction policy for completed and errored job records (e.g., a max-entries LRU cap or a TTL-based sweep) and add an AC or scoped-out note so implementers do not leave an unbounded accumulation.
findings.finding: Completed and errored job records are accumulated in the in-memory job dict with no eviction or retirement policy; the plan specifies neither a max-size bound nor a TTL-based cleanup, so a long-running service leaks memory monotonically.
findings.location: What section — Service, 'Job state is in-memory (a restart loses queued jobs; clients re-submit — cert minting is idempotent)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bounded `OrderedDict` (e.g., max 1 000 entries, evict oldest completed) or a per-record TTL (e.g., 1 hour after reaching a terminal state) and document it in the 'Service' bullet; add a corresponding AC asserting the store does not grow beyond the bound.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate in the plan that existing tests referencing `transition_close._verdict_manifest` must be updated (or confirmed to call only through the public `close_ticket` entry point) when it is extracted to `sign_completion_verdict`.
findings.finding: The plan proposes extracting _verdict_manifest from transition_close.py into a reusable sign_completion_verdict function, but does not enumerate updating tests that currently test _verdict_manifest (or the transition_close close-path) directly. The existing close-gate tests in the test suite call into transition_close._completion_precheck / _verdict_manifest either directly or by exercising the close path; if the function is renamed/moved without updating those test callsites, the test suite will break.
findings.location: Service section / 'this story EXTRACTS that pair into a reusable sign_completion_verdict(result, ticket_id, repo_root) used by BOTH transition_close.py ... and this worker'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'A raised `LLMError`/`LLMUnavailableError` maps to the job's transport status — never a fabricated verdict' and specifies `error.class = "llm_unavailable"` for `LLMUnavailableError`. For the `plan-review` kind this premise is false: `rebar.llm.review_plan` absorbs `LLMUnavailableError` internally (inside `produce_plan_review_verdict`, which calls `_degraded_plan_review_verdict` and returns `INDETERMINATE`) and its docstring explicitly states 'Raises only on a hard context-assembly failure; an unavailable LLM degrades to a DET-only review.' The worker's `except LLMUnavailableError` block will never fire for a `plan-review` job — LLM failures will reach the service as `status='completed', verdict='INDETERMINATE'` rather than `status='error'` as the plan intends. Only `verify_completion` raises `LLMError` to the caller.
findings.location: What section — 'Per-kind dispatch + native verdicts' paragraph; and AC 'Per-kind verdict mapping' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: For plan-review: inspect the returned verdict's `verdict` field and `coverage.llm_ran` rather than catching a raised exception. Map `INDETERMINATE` with `coverage.llm_ran=False` (systemic LLM failure) to `status='error', error.class='llm_unavailable'`; map `INDETERMINATE` with `coverage.llm_ran=True` (partial run) to `status='completed', verdict='INDETERMINATE'`. Only `verify_completion` needs the raised-exception catch.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `transition_close.py` refactor (extraction of `sign_completion_verdict`) to the Scope/In list so the deliverable is unambiguously in-scope.
findings.finding: The 'Scope / In:' list omits the extraction of `sign_completion_verdict` from `transition_close.py`, which is described as in-scope work in the 'What' section — creating a cross-section contradiction an implementer must resolve.
findings.location: Scope section ('In:' list) vs. What section ('verify_completion has NO sign parameter … this story EXTRACTS that pair into a reusable sign_completion_verdict')
findings.suggested_fix: Append to Scope/In: 'extraction of `sign_completion_verdict` from `transition_close.py` (behavior-preserving refactor enabling reuse by both the service worker and the existing close-gate path).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that runs the existing `transition_close.py` close-gate signing path against a fixture and asserts the resulting SIGNATURE event shape and envelope are identical before and after the `sign_completion_verdict` extraction.
findings.finding: The `sign_completion_verdict` extraction modifies `transition_close.py` (a live close-gate path) but no AC verifies that `transition_close.py` produces identical results after the refactor — an orphaned deliverable with no covering criterion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all ACs) vs. What section ('this story EXTRACTS that pair … used by BOTH transition_close.py (behavior unchanged) and this worker')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the `transition_close.py` refactor (e.g., keep the old inline signing block behind a feature flag or expand-contract: add the new function alongside, prove it, then remove the old block) and add a regression AC.
findings.finding: The extraction of `sign_completion_verdict` from `transition_close.py` changes an existing behavioral unit (the live close-gate signing path) without a stated rollback path, expand-contract sequence, or regression gate — leaving the plan's 'behavior unchanged' claim unverified and unrecoverable if wrong.
findings.location: What section ('this story EXTRACTS that pair into a reusable sign_completion_verdict … transition_close.py (behavior unchanged)')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `verify.opcert_remote_url` to whichever config-schema or user-facing configuration reference documents `VerifyConfig` fields (e.g. docs/configuration.md or CLAUDE.md), alongside its semantics ('unset → local-only; non-null → remote cert endpoint') and the 'absence never blocks local operation' guarantee.
findings.finding: The new `verify.opcert_remote_url` key in `VerifyConfig` is user-facing configuration (lives in the project's config file, e.g. `.rebar/verify.yaml`), but the plan only arranges for env-var documentation (`scripts/gen_env_registry.py` → `docs/env-vars.md`). No update to any configuration schema reference, user guide, or `VerifyConfig` field inventory is mentioned, leaving the config-file surface undocumented.
findings.location: What / Client section — '`verify.opcert_remote_url` (new `VerifyConfig` key, `str | None = None`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability section (or ACs) specifying structured logging per job state transition, a /health or /ready endpoint, and at minimum a logged error count per error.class so operators can detect and triage systematic failures.
findings.finding: No observability plan is specified for the new service: no structured logging, no metrics, no health/readiness endpoint, and no alerting on job failures, SSM errors, or git-fetch failures.
findings.location: What — Service section (entire); Acceptance Criteria

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify an explicit inner timeout (e.g. `git clone --depth 1 --timeout 60`) and at least one retry with backoff for the git-fetch and SSM-fetch operations, independent of the outer 900 s job timeout.
findings.finding: The git-fetch operations (tickets branch and review-remote main tip) have no explicit timeout or retry policy. A hung or slow remote blocks the single serial worker for the full 900 s job timeout, starving all subsequent queued jobs.
findings.location: What — Authoritative fetch section: 'the worker fetches the tickets branch … and the review remote's main tip … with its own read-only credentials'
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap git clone/fetch in a `asyncio.wait_for(..., timeout=60)` coroutine and add a single SSM retry with 1 s backoff; map git-fetch failure to a new `error.class='fetch_error'` or fold into `'internal'` with a clear log message.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the client-side polling strategy: maximum total wait (suggested: slightly above 900 s), inter-poll backoff, and behavior on non-terminal HTTP errors or 404 (job lost on restart) — and add a corresponding AC.
findings.finding: The client `rebar remote-cert` polling loop has no specified client-side timeout or give-up policy; if the server job stalls (e.g., the background worker hangs before the 900 s job timeout fires, or the network intermittently fails), the client polls indefinitely with no user-visible progress or escape.
findings.location: Client section — 'submits the job, polls to a terminal status'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a client-side hard timeout (e.g., `REBAR_OPCERT_POLL_TIMEOUT_SECONDS`, default 960 s), exponential backoff with a cap, and explicit handling of 404 (print 'job not found — service may have restarted; resubmit' then exit non-zero). Document these in the Client section and add an AC that asserts the client exits within the timeout even if the server never reaches a terminal status.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `sign_completion_verdict` extraction meets Rule-of-Three (≥3 call-sites) before creating a shared function; if only 2 consumers exist at ship time, consider inlining the 2-step `_verdict_manifest` + `sign_manifest` call in the worker instead.
findings.finding: The plan proposes extracting a new `sign_completion_verdict(result, ticket_id, repo_root)` function from `transition_close.py`'s `_verdict_manifest` + `sign_manifest` call to share between `transition_close.py` and the opcert service worker — exactly 2 call-sites. The Rule-of-Three threshold (≥3 existing call-sites) is not met, making this a premature abstraction.
findings.location: What section — 'Signing = the producer seam, once' paragraph: 'the worker invokes the same producer function, not a bespoke signer … this story EXTRACTS that pair into a reusable sign_completion_verdict(result, ticket_id, repo_root) used by BOTH transition_close.py … and this worker'
findings.suggested_fix: In the opcert worker, call `_verdict_manifest(result, ticket_id, repo_root)` + `signing.sign_manifest(ticket_id, manifest, kind='completion-verifier', repo_root=repo_root)` directly (same 2-step sequence already in transition_close.py), deferring the abstraction until a third consumer materializes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief contract description of `opcert.opcert_from_record` to the plan (input: the attestation record dict from `show_ticket(...)["attestations"][kind]`; return: at minimum the `envelope` bytes/string and the bound fields the response carries), so the worker step is implementable without reading the unmerged Gerrit change.
findings.finding: `opcert.opcert_from_record` is invoked in the plan as the named mechanism for decoding the attestation envelope from `show_ticket(ticket_id)["attestations"][kind]` after the signing run, but the function does not exist anywhere in the codebase (`src/rebar/attest/opcert.py` is absent) and its contract — input type, return shape, what 'bound fields' it exposes — is never described in the plan. The caller (the service worker) depends on a black-box interface whose signature and return value are entirely unspecified.
findings.location: What section — Service — 'Stateless / store-read-only' paragraph: 'opcert.opcert_from_record decodes its envelope + bound fields'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Stateless / store-read-only' paragraph, expand the `opcert_from_record` reference to specify its signature: e.g. `opcert.opcert_from_record(record) -> OpcertRecord` where `OpcertRecord` has at minimum `.envelope: str` (the encoded DSSE) and `.material_fingerprint: str` and `.merged_log_commit: str`. Even a one-sentence summary ('returns a dataclass/dict with envelope, material_fingerprint, merged_log_commit extracted from the SIGNATURE event record') would make the worker step independently implementable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `remote-cert` to `src/rebar/_cli/help/overview.txt` (the `rebar --help` listing) and update the corresponding CLI-help golden fixture.
findings.finding: The plan enumerates updating INTERCEPT_COMMANDS in gen_cli_reference.py and adding a help file under src/rebar/_cli/help/, but does NOT mention updating src/rebar/_cli/help/overview.txt (the rebar --help listing). The known_subcommands() function derives the set of known commands from .txt filenames in that directory; an intercept arm that fires before the unknown-subcommand guard does not need to be in known_subcommands() to avoid rejection, but overview.txt is the user-visible listing of all commands and is a pinned golden (tests/golden/cli_help). Adding remote-cert.txt without updating overview.txt means 'rebar --help' silently omits the new command, and the golden test for overview will pass with a stale listing.
findings.location: Client section / 'the argv[0] == intercept ladder in src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py gains the remote-cert arm; a help file is added under src/rebar/_cli/help/'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line for `remote-cert` to `src/rebar/_cli/help/overview.txt` in the same commit that adds the intercept arm, and regenerate the pinned golden under `tests/golden/cli_help/`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` to the in-scope file list (with a service entry for the opcert app) or explicitly call out that compose wiring is deferred to the AWS IaC story and explain how the Dockerfile AC test is driven without it.
findings.finding: The plan names infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert as in-scope but does not mention infra/compose/docker-compose.yml as a file requiring updating. The existing review-bot service is declared in docker-compose.yml; adding a new Dockerfile without a corresponding service definition leaves the container non-runnable in the local compose environment. While full AWS deploy is out-of-scope, docker-compose.yml is the local integration harness referenced by the review_bot pattern this plan mirrors.
findings.location: Scope section / 'In: ... Dockerfile' and 'Container image: infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State why the envelope must be read back from the ephemeral store via `show_ticket` + `opcert_from_record` rather than captured directly from `sign_opcert_manifest`'s return value — if `sign_opcert_manifest` returns the envelope, use the direct path; if not, document the constraint so the implementer knows the store-readback is load-bearing.
findings.finding: The plan specifies envelope extraction via a store read-back (`show_ticket(ticket_id)["attestations"][kind]` in the ephemeral workspace followed by `opcert.opcert_from_record`) after `review_plan`/`sign_completion_verdict` returns, but gives no rationale for why the envelope cannot be captured directly from the return value of `sign_opcert_manifest`. The store read-back introduces a dependency on the ephemeral workspace reducer correctly materializing the just-written SIGNATURE event before the worker reads it back — an extra failure surface (reduce failure, timing, or store misconfiguration in the ephemeral clone) compared to using the signing call's own return value. The absence of a stated rationale for this design choice leaves the implementer without guidance on whether the roundabout path is intentional (e.g. because `sign_opcert_manifest` does not return the envelope) or accidental complexity.
findings.location: What > Service — '…reads back the freshly-appended record by the NAMED mechanism — show_ticket(ticket_id)["attestations"][kind] in the ephemeral workspace (the reduced record of the just-written SIGNATURE event; opcert.opcert_from_record decodes its envelope + bound fields)…'
findings.suggested_fix: If `sign_opcert_manifest` returns the encoded DSSE envelope (as its name suggests it should), capture it from the return value: `record = sign_opcert_manifest(...); envelope = record['envelope']` — no store read-back needed. If the seam does not return the envelope, add that constraint to the dependency spec and add a sentence here explaining why.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability contract to the plan: specify a `/health` liveness endpoint, structured-log format (e.g., JSON to stdout for CloudWatch), and at minimum one alarm requirement (e.g., job error-rate or worker-timeout alarm) so the sibling IaC story has a concrete spec to implement against.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new internet-reachable FastAPI service (`src/rebar/opcert_service/`) but specifies no observability contract for it: no structured-log format or log-level discipline, no metrics or health-check endpoint, and no alarms. The sibling IaC/deploy story will wire up the runtime environment, but if observability requirements are not stated in this story, the deploy story has no spec to implement against and the service ships dark.
findings.location: What section — Service bullet; Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers logging/health); Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet under the Service section: '`GET /health` returns 200 `{status: "ok"}` and is excluded from the guard check. The service logs JSON to stdout (timestamp, level, job_id, kind, status, duration_ms). The deploy story MUST create a CloudWatch alarm on `status=error` job completions exceeding N in M minutes.' Add a corresponding AC: 'A `/health` endpoint returns 200 with no guard header required; service logs include job_id and terminal status as structured JSON fields.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` is the exact env-var name the store's push logic reads (cite the source file and line), or name the correct variable; add it to the env-var registry so the CI env-var drift gate covers it.
findings.finding: The plan relies on `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` to prevent the ephemeral workspace from pushing to the shared tickets branch, but this env-var name is not verified to exist as a recognised config knob in the codebase. If the env var is simply ignored or the variable name differs from what the store actually reads, the no-push invariant silently fails.
findings.location: Service section — 'Stateless / store-read-only' paragraph; AC 'Server never pushes'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the interface contract of `opcert.opcert_from_record` inline (or by reference to the substrate spec): what arguments it accepts and what it returns (specifically: the type/shape of the `envelope` field it yields), so the worker's return-path and AC1's test double can be written without consulting unmerged code.
findings.finding: `opcert.opcert_from_record` is named as the mechanism the worker uses to decode the signed envelope from the ephemeral workspace's `show_ticket` result, but the function's interface (signature and return shape) is never specified in this plan, and the function does not exist in the current codebase (it is in unmerged substrate Gerrit 649/650/660).
findings.location: What section — 'Stateless / store-read-only' paragraph: 'opcert.opcert_from_record decodes its envelope + bound fields'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback for the `completion-verifier` PASS path when `show_ticket(ticket_id)["attestations"][kind]` returns no record in the ephemeral workspace (e.g. treat as `status='error', class='internal'`) so the worker's behavior is deterministic.
findings.finding: The `completion-verifier` PASS path requires reading back the signed attestation via `show_ticket(ticket_id)["attestations"]["completion-verifier"]` in the ephemeral workspace after signing, then decoding it with `opcert.opcert_from_record`. No fallback is specified if this read-back finds no record (e.g. the ephemeral workspace's store hasn't replayed the just-written SIGNATURE event, or the key `"completion-verifier"` is absent from `attestations`).
findings.location: What section — 'Stateless / store-read-only' paragraph; AC5 ('the server appends no tickets-branch event')

findings.finding: The plan creates `src/rebar/_commands/remote_cert.py` and the CLI command `rebar remote-cert` but does not enumerate the required wiring in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` (a new `if argv[0] == 'remote-cert':` intercept-ladder entry) nor the `src/rebar/_cli/help/remote-cert.txt` help file that `known_subcommands()` uses to recognize the command. Without these, `rebar remote-cert` will be rejected as an unknown subcommand at runtime.
findings.location: Client section / `src/rebar/_commands/remote_cert.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope/In list: `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` (new `if argv[0] == 'remote-cert':` intercept arm) and `src/rebar/_cli/help/remote-cert.txt` (pinned help text for `known_subcommands()`).

findings.finding: `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` is an unenumerated consumer: it parses the `argv[0] ==` intercept ladder in `_cli/__init__.py` and does a strict set-equality check against its curated `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` dict. Adding a `remote-cert` arm to the ladder without also adding it to `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` raises `ValueError` and fails `make test`.
findings.location: Client section / `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` to Scope/In with a one-line description entry for `remote-cert` in the `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` dict.

findings.finding: All new `REBAR_OPCERT_*` environment variables read inside `src/rebar/opcert_service/` will be auto-detected by `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` (its scan root is `src/rebar`), causing the CI env-var drift gate to fail `make test` until `docs/env-vars.md` is regenerated. The plan neither mentions regenerating `docs/env-vars.md` nor lists it as an edit target.
findings.location: Service section (env vars: REBAR_OPCERT_JOB_TIMEOUT_SECONDS, REBAR_OPCERT_REVIEW_REMOTE_URL, REBAR_OPCERT_TICKETS_REMOTE_URL, REBAR_OPCERT_KEY_PATH, REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID, REBAR_OPCERT_GUARD)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope/In: '`docs/env-vars.md` (regenerate with `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py` after adding the REBAR_OPCERT_* reads)'.

findings.finding: The `completion-verifier` dispatch path's envelope-extraction mechanism silently depends on the producer seam adding `sign=True` support to `verify_completion`, but the live code confirms `verify_completion` has no `sign` parameter (unlike `review_plan`'s `sign: bool = True`). The plan says the seam 'must land first' but never confirms that the seam story adds a signing path to `verify_completion`. If the seam only signs `review_plan`, the CV dispatch produces no SIGNATURE event in the ephemeral workspace, and the envelope extraction step ('the worker extracts the envelope from the workspace's freshly-appended record') fails silently or crashes — breaking the CV path entirely.
findings.location: What > Service > Per-kind dispatch; What > Service > Signing = the producer seam
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the 'Signing = the producer seam' section: 'The seam story MUST add signing support to both `review_plan` (already has `sign: bool = True`) AND `verify_completion` (currently unsigned); the CV dispatch path requires a freshly-appended SIGNATURE event in the ephemeral workspace to extract the envelope.'

findings.finding: The envelope extraction protocol for completed jobs is under-specified. The plan says 'the worker extracts the envelope from the workspace's freshly-appended record', but does not name the store event type, the dict field, or the lookup mechanism for either kind — and for `completion-verifier` the live `verify_completion()` has no `sign` parameter and its return schema has no `signature` or `envelope` field, so the extraction path depends entirely on the (not-yet-landed) producer-seam story's shape, which is not described here.
findings.location: What section — Service, 'Stateless / store-read-only — reconciled with the seam's `sign=True`'; also 'the worker extracts the envelope from the workspace's freshly-appended record'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
